briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for July 11, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Unprecedented Climate Crisis, Threatening Global Life Systems
sections.content: According to reports today, the global crisis caused by climate change is intensifying. In Europe, a tragedy occurred with over 4,700 deaths due to unprecedented heatwaves [6, 43, 59], and UK parliamentarians are recognizing the severity of the situation, urging the government for an emergency TV briefing on the climate crisis [47, 54]. These extreme weather events are having a devastating impact on crops both domestically and internationally, causing not only damage to garlic crops but also over $20 billion in annual crop losses worldwide, leading to a surge in coffee prices [4, 5, 31]. Furthermore, catastrophic mortality events are being reported across marine ecosystems, including a halving of the Pacific gray whale population, and threats to biodiversity are accelerating, with concerns raised about the spread of invasive species and the potential reduction in squid brain size [30, 32, 35, 49, 53].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic and International Practices and Innovations Towards a Carbon-Neutral Society
sections.content: Amidst this crisis, domestic and international efforts towards a carbon-neutral society are actively continuing. South Korea has strengthened forest cooperation with Mongolia to combat desertification and respond to climate change [1, 28], and the Climate Change Center has proposed a 'citizen-participatory climate-safe city' model [2]. Various domestic institutions and local governments are implementing carbon neutrality in diverse ways, including improving the thermal efficiency of old rental housing [7], eco-friendly moss ball making experiences [3], carbon-neutral flea markets [10], and campaigns to spread a culture of carbon-neutral practices [15]. Notably, Jeonnam Gwangju Special City has launched a 12.5 billion won carbon-neutral pilot project to convert waste into clean energy [11], and international cooperation and efforts are continuing, with Ho Chi Minh City in Vietnam exploring financial solutions for the development of carbon-neutral industrial complexes [14, 20].

sections.title: ⚠️ Realistic Challenges and Policy Solutions for Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy is essential, but it also faces realistic challenges. Particularly, the Honam and Jeju regions are reportedly trapped in a renewable energy grid bottleneck, highlighting the urgent need for integrated strategies for power generation and industrial siting [18, 21, 23, 24, 27]. To address these grid constraints, the establishment of ESS (Energy Storage Systems) is being actively initiated [16, 21], but delays in the disclosure of the 12th Basic Plan for Electricity Supply and Demand are raising concerns about a contraction in renewable energy investment [22]. Furthermore, contrary to the spirit of 'just transition,' non-regular workers at Taean Power Plant are criticizing the joint development of Taean offshore wind power, making social consensus and ensuring fairness critical issues [25]. Meanwhile, research in the international community suggests that trees may not be as effective as once thought in slowing climate change, leading to growing calls for new directions in renewable energy supply chains [26, 39].

sections.title: 📰 Social, Economic, and Policy Repercussions Caused by Climate Change
sections.content: Climate change is causing widespread repercussions across society and the economy, extending beyond mere environmental issues. The International Court of Justice (ICJ) has issued an advisory opinion on climate change and cyber operations, emphasizing the importance of legal and policy responses [33]. It is also expanding into human rights issues, with investigations underway into the link between forced displacement and statelessness due to climate change [38], and the possibility that climate change could reshape approaches to antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in animal diseases has been raised [40]. Interestingly, an analysis shows that while Generation Z constantly thinks about climate change, their voting behavior tends not to align with these thoughts, raising questions about the connection between climate education and political participation [34]. Furthermore, reports from the United States indicate that the Trump administration spent vast sums to suppress clean energy and support the coal industry, suggesting that international discussions on the direction of climate policy will intensify [55].

briefing.closing: This concludes today's climate briefing. I would like to reiterate that sustained climate action and policy efforts are key to overcoming the global crisis, and I hope to deliver more positive news in our next briefing.

articles.title: Pacific gray whales facing ‘catastrophic’ die-off as climate crisis hits food supply
articles.description: Trump administration urged to relist a species in ‘very, very serious trouble’ under Endangered Species ActClimate change is driving a gray whale “catastrophic mortality event” in the Pacific Ocean as melting sea ice depletes food sources and the animals starve, environmental groups warn.Meanwhile, a range of other issues, like ship strikes, oil spills, microplastic pollution, algal blooms and Russian harvesting are also probably contributing to the die-off that has nearly halved the whales’ est

summary.phenomenon: Environmental groups warn that Pacific gray whales are facing a 'catastrophic mortality event,' with their population nearly halved.
summary.cause: Reduced sea ice due to climate change is depleting the whales' food sources, leading to starvation. Furthermore, various factors such as ship strikes, oil spills, microplastic pollution, algal blooms, and Russian harvesting are also likely contributing to the mortality.
summary.outlook: Environmental groups are urging the Trump administration to relist the species under the Endangered Species Act.

summary.phenomenon: The UK is experiencing another heatwave, described as the most 'insidious threat' to society. Last November, a national emergency briefing was held in Westminster Hall, where nine experts provided assessments on the scale of changes needed for national adaptation.
summary.outlook: UK Members of Parliament are urging the government to host a televised national emergency briefing regarding the climate crisis.

articles.title: ‘He’s forcing higher bills’: Trump spends billions to kill clean energy and keep coal alive
articles.description: Critics accuse president of ‘fattening the wallets of his cronies’ as working Americans face higher energy ratesThe Trump administration has directly spent $2.7bn of taxpayer money on its crusade against wind power while pouring $1.125bn into boosting coal, which critics say is pushing up Americans’ bills.They say the moves are evidence that the president aims to serve fossil-fuel companies like those which donated record sums to his presidential campaign, rather than the working-class Americans

summary.phenomenon: The Trump administration directly spent $2.7 billion of taxpayer money on its opposition to wind power and $1.125 billion to support the coal industry.
summary.cause: Critics argue that these actions are aimed at helping fossil fuel companies that donated significant sums to President Trump's presidential campaign.
summary.outlook: Critics contend that these policies are increasing energy rates for working-class Americans.

articles.original_title: We know how to mourn other humans – but what about ecological grief?
articles.description: In Iceland, people commemorated its first glacier formally declared lost to climate change. Western culture needs more of these ritualsI remember interviewing a North Atlantic right whale expert years ago. He was a practical, science-minded man. But as we discussed a female whale that had lost her calf, he became visibly emotional. She had lost the previous one, too, struck by a ship. He seemed almost embarrassed by the depth of his feeling.I wasn’t surprised. I found his grief honorable. Contin

summary.phenomenon: In Iceland, a ceremony was held to commemorate its first glacier formally declared lost to climate change.
summary.cause: Iceland's glacier has disappeared due to climate change.
summary.outlook: Western culture needs more of these commemorative rituals, suggesting a discussion on how to address ecological grief.
